notes: Skill adds +0.83 pass rate improvement across all 5 evals — with_skill achieves 100% on every eval vs. 0–33% without skill.
Process assertions (worktree creation, reference file reads, uv pipeline pattern) are the primary discriminators — without_skill fails all of them consistently.

Eval 2 (help-invocation) and Eval 4 (specific-packages): the 'no-worktree' and 'targets specific packages' assertions both pass vacuously for without_skill because the agent does nothing or the prompt itself constrains the scope — these are non-discriminating assertions.

Eval 3 (update-latest): the 'latest versions' scope inference passes for without_skill — this is natural language inference that Claude does well without skill guidance, suggesting that scope inference may not need explicit skill instructions.

The skill adds significant overhead: ~74.8s and ~28,200 tokens per run vs. baseline. This is expected given worktree creation, reference file reads, GHSA severity lookups, sequential update loops, and commit/PR creation.
Eval 2 (help-invocation) is the fastest with_skill run (22s, 8500 tokens) since it just displays help and asks a question — no actual package operations.
High time variance for with_skill (stddev 47.7s) reflects the wide range of workflows: simple help display (22s) vs. full security audit with GHSA lookups (145s).

Severity filtering (Critical+High default, Critical-only explicit) is a key differentiator: without skill, agents lack the GHSA lookup workflow and cannot apply structured severity filters — pip-audit has no built-in --severity flag.
